data.title: How many Fed rate cuts in 2026?
Who will Trump nominate as Fed Chair?
What will Fed Rate hit before 2027?
Which countries will Trump make new trade deals with before 2027?
Will Trump create a tariff dividend by March 31?

data.data:
[EVIDENCE]: [2026-03-04] WeatherSentinel alert indicates Côte d'Ivoire weekly precipitation at 28.6mm, slightly below the 30.0mm threshold, occurring during the VEGETATIVE stage with a NEUTRAL direction. [2026-02-26] Reports indicate Côte d'Ivoire may end March with 200,000 tons of unsold cocoa due to price gaps. [2026-02-27] NY and London cocoa hit multi-year lows following an ICCO report raising the global surplus estimate to 75,000 MT. [2026-03-01] Côte d'Ivoire officially launched its mid-crop season early to manage the surplus.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis contained a hallucinated claim regarding shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, which was not supported by the provided evidence. Correcting for this, the analysis must rely strictly on the given data. The WeatherSentinel alert flags a minor moisture deficit (28.6mm against a 30mm threshold). However, because the crop is in the VEGETATIVE stage and the directional signal is NEUTRAL, this slight shortfall poses minimal immediate threat to yields compared to potential stress during flowering or pod-filling. This minor agronomic factor is vastly outweighed by bearish supply fundamentals. Specifically, Côte d'Ivoire is holding approximately 200,000 tons of unsold beans and has launched its mid-crop early to manage inventory. Furthermore, the ICCO has raised the global surplus estimate to 75,000 MT, driving prices to multi-year lows. Without evidence of severe crop degradation, the overwhelming physical market oversupply dictates a strongly bearish outlook.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: - [2026-03-03] Fed officials Kashkari and Williams signaled a hawkish shift due to Middle East conflict and energy price shocks, pushing money market expectations for a rate cut from July to September 2026. (Bloomberg Law, Reuters)
- [2026-03-03] The US Dollar Index (DXY) rallied to a 3.25-month high near 99.10, and the 10-year Treasury yield spiked to 4.107%. (Barchart, Times-Online)
- [2026-03-04] Cocoa prices rose 1.88% to $3,071.55 USD/T, recovering from late February lows due to technical buying and shipping cost concerns linked to Strait of Hormuz disruptions. (Trading Economics)
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis correctly identified the competing forces but lacked precision regarding the transmission mechanism of the shipping disruptions. While West African and South American cocoa do not directly transit the Strait of Hormuz, the Middle East conflict has triggered global energy price shocks and broader systemic freight cost increases. Trading Economics explicitly cites these global shipping cost concerns and technical buying as the drivers for cocoa's 1.88% rebound. Conversely, these same inflationary energy shocks have prompted a hawkish pivot from Fed officials Kashkari and Williams (Bloomberg Law, Reuters), delaying rate cut expectations to September 2026. Consequently, the DXY surged to 99.10 and 10-year yields hit 4.107% (Barchart, Times-Online). This strong dollar environment acts as a severe macroeconomic headwind, making dollar-denominated cocoa more expensive for foreign buyers and typically depressing demand. The market is currently caught in a tug-of-war between bearish macro/currency pressures and bullish cost-push/technical factors, firmly justifying a neutral near-term outlook.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: NEUTRAL]

[EVIDENCE]: 1) [2026-03-04] Ivory Coast government committed to purchasing 100,000 MT of residual stock at the guaranteed 2,800 CFA price to prevent threatened strikes and administrative blockades by farmers in Duekoue and San Pedro. 2) [2026-03-02] Ghana's COCOBOD reported a debt of GHS 32.5 billion, alongside a GH₵30 billion working capital shortfall that forced 10-20% executive pay cuts. 3) [2026-03-01] Côte d'Ivoire officially advanced its mid-crop season to March, slashing farmgate prices to 800-1,000 CFA/kg. 4) [Raw Research] These policy shifts have created inverted smuggling incentives and elevated supply-side risks due to the financial instability of state-run boards.
[ANALYSIS]: The previous analysis suffered from confirmation bias by framing inherently bearish physical supply indicators—such as the early launch of the mid-crop and the existence of 100,000 MT of residual stock—exclusively as bullish supply chain failures. A more objective evaluation reveals a market torn between bearish physical availability and bullish institutional risks. On the bearish side, Côte d'Ivoire's ability to advance the mid-crop to March 1 and slash farmgate prices to 800-1,000 CFA/kg indicates immediate physical supply is coming online. However, severe structural risks threaten the delivery of this supply. Ghana's COCOBOD is facing a severe liquidity crisis with a GHS 32.5 billion debt and GH₵30 billion shortfall (March 2), crippling its procurement capacity. In Côte d'Ivoire, the drastic price cuts have sparked strike threats, forcing a March 4 emergency government intervention to buy residual stock at the old 2,800 CFA price to prevent blockades. Consequently, while physical beans exist, financial instability, civil unrest, and inverted smuggling incentives severely threaten commercial export channels, resulting in a highly volatile but fundamentally mixed outlook.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: NEUTRAL]

[EVIDENCE]: - Ivory Coast cocoa arrivals: 1.335M MT as of Mar 1, down 3.7% YoY (Reuters/MarketScreener, 2026-03-02). - Weekly deliveries Feb 23-Mar 1: 15k MT Abidjan (up from prior 12k), 13k MT San Pedro (CocoaIntel, 2026-03-02). - Europe Q4 2025 grindings: -8.3% YoY to 304k MT, 12-yr low (ECA, 2026-01-15). - ICCO 2024/25: 75k MT surplus (prod +8.4%, grindings -4.2%) (CocoaIntel, 2026-03-03). - Shipping/insurance costs up for cocoa exports due to Hormuz halt (Barchart/Nasdaq, 2026-03-02), though primary W. Africa-Europe routes less directly impacted.
[ANALYSIS]: Supply resilient (weekly Abidjan deliveries up to 15k MT, cumulative -3.7% YoY mild) amid ICCO-projected 75k MT global surplus (prod growth outpacing grindings decline), while Europe grindings signal weak demand (-8.3% YoY Q4 low). Shipping costs rising per source, adding minor export pressure, but no evidence of physical bottlenecks (e.g., congestion, queues at Abidjan/Tema). Overall excess supply supports bearish price outlook; prior analysis sound but adds nuance on shipping route relevance to avoid over-causation.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.8
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: 1. [2026-03-03] ICE cocoa inventories rose to a 6.5-month high of 2,200,058 bags (Source: Barchart). 2. [2026-03-03] Cocoa prices settled at 3,015 USD/T, representing a 26.21% decline over the past month (Source: Trading Economics). 3. [2026-03-02] ICCO revised end-of-season stocks upward to 1.347 million tonnes, a 5.9% year-on-year increase (Source: CocoaIntel). 4. [2026-02-27] Hedgepoint Global Markets projected a global cocoa surplus of 365,000 tons for the 2025/26 crop year (Source: Trading Economics). 5. [2026-03-02] Ivory Coast cocoa arrivals for 2025/26 reached 1.335 million metric tons, down 3.7% year-over-year (Source: CocoaIntel).
[ANALYSIS]: SELF-CRITIQUE: Three weaknesses are identified in the prior analysis. First, confirmation bias: the prior analysis systematically amplified every bearish data point while dismissing the Ivory Coast arrivals decline (-3.7% YoY) with a single sentence. A genuinely balanced analysis must quantify why 1.335 MMT in arrivals is insufficient to offset the surplus — not merely assert it. If arrivals continue to decline materially into mid-season, the supply overhang could shrink faster than the Hedgepoint/StoneX projections anticipate. Second, false causation risk: the claim that 'international importers are rejecting elevated West African farm-gate prices, causing beans to back up into exchange warehouses' is a plausible mechanism but is not directly evidenced in the cited data packet — it is an inferential narrative, not a confirmed causal chain. This should be labeled as a working hypothesis, not a structural conclusion. Third, confidence inflation: a 0.92 confidence score is difficult to justify when (a) the surplus projection range is wide (287,000–365,000 MT), (b) one key supply indicator (arrivals) is pointing opposite to the bearish thesis, and (c) cocoa prices have already declined 26% in one month, meaning a significant portion of the bearish thesis may already be priced in — reducing expected forward edge. REVISED ASSESSMENT: The weight of evidence remains bearish. ICE certified stocks have risen in a consistent, stepwise trajectory to a 6.5-month high (Barchart, 2026-03-03), the ICCO has confirmed a 5.9% YoY increase in end-of-season stocks to 1.347 MMT (CocoaIntel, 2026-03-02), and independent surplus projections from Hedgepoint (365,000 MT) and StoneX (287,000 MT) converge on a meaningful supply overhang for 2025/26. These are corroborated by hard price action: a 26.21% decline to 3,015 USD/T (Trading Economics, 2026-03-03). However, two risks temper confidence: (1) Ivory Coast arrivals are running 3.7% below prior-year pace, which if sustained could narrow the surplus meaningfully by season-end; and (2) at 3,015 USD/T — down from peaks above 10,000 USD/T in 2024 — markets may have already discounted much of the known bearish information, limiting incremental downside unless stocks continue building at the current pace. The bearish thesis is structurally intact but not risk-free, and confidence is revised downward to MODERATE to reflect these unresolved offsets.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BEARISH]

[EVIDENCE]: - [2026-03-03] Fed officials Kashkari and Williams signaled hawkish shift due to Middle East conflict and energy price shocks, delaying rate cut expectations to September 2026 (Financial Post, Bloomberg News, Bloomberg Law, Reuters). - [2026-03-03] US Dollar Index (DXY) surged to 3.25-month high of 99.13; 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.056%-4.107% (Barchart, Morningstar, Times-Online). - [2026-03-04] Cocoa prices rose 1.88% to $3,071.55 USD/T, recovering from late February low of $2,888, due to technical buying and shipping cost concerns from Strait of Hormuz disruptions (Trading Economics). - [2026-03-02] Crude oil surged over 8% on US-Israeli strikes on Iran halting Strait of Hormuz traffic (Expana Markets).
[ANALYSIS]: Original analysis shows mild recency bias (all data March 2-4, 2026) but no strong confirmation bias or false causation; hawkish Fed/DXY strength (99.13) and higher yields (4.09%) exert bearish pressure on USD cocoa via elevated costs for non-US buyers and tighter conditions, yet Hormuz disruptions spike freight/fuel/insurance, enabling short-term cost-push rebound (+1.88%) despite surplus outlook—no direct cocoa supply/demand shift observed. Balanced counter-forces justify neutral short-term view; long-term macro may dominate absent escalation.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.55
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: NEUTRAL]

[EVIDENCE]: - [2026-03-04] Cocoa prices rose 1.88% to $3,071.55 USD/T, recovering from late February lows due to technical buying and shipping cost concerns. (Trading Economics)
- [2026-03-02] NY Cocoa futures (CCK26) closed up 4.61% as the war in Iran halted traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, spiking global shipping and fuel costs. (Nasdaq)
- [2026-03-03] The US Dollar Index (DXY) rallied to a 3.25-month high of 99.10, undercutting New York cocoa futures while boosting London cocoa due to a weaker Pound. (Barchart)
- [2026-03-03] Fed's Kashkari expressed diminished confidence in a 2026 rate cut, shifting market expectations for a cut from July to September 2026. (Bloomberg Law, Trading Economics)
[ANALYSIS]: Cocoa prices are navigating a conflict between severe geopolitical supply chain shocks and bearish macroeconomic headwinds. Bullish momentum is currently dominating, with NY Cocoa futures surging 4.61% on March 2 (Nasdaq) and an additional 1.88% to $3,071.55 USD/T on March 4 (Trading Economics). This price action is directly tied to the war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has spiked global freight and fuel costs. Conversely, macroeconomic conditions present a strong bearish counterweight: hawkish comments from the Fed's Kashkari pushed rate cut expectations to September 2026 (Bloomberg Law), driving the US Dollar Index to a 3.25-month high of 99.10 (Barchart). While a strong DXY fundamentally undercuts USD-denominated cocoa, the immediate, acute cost-push inflation from the Middle East conflict is currently overpowering these macro headwinds, resulting in a net-bullish short-term trend. The previous analysis contained a hallucination regarding 10-year yields (4.09%) that was not in the source text, failed to directly attribute claims to the provided sources, and incorrectly labeled a clearly upward price trend as neutral. These logical and sourcing errors have been corrected in this revision.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.65
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: BULLISH]

[EVIDENCE]: - [2026-03-04] Cocoa prices rose 1.88% to $3,071.55 USD/T, recovering from near three-year lows hit in late February. (Trading Economics)
- [2026-03-03] US Dollar Index (DXY) surged to a 3.25-month high of 99.13 as traders scaled back Fed easing expectations. (Barchart)
- [2026-03-04] The 10-year US Treasury yield rose to 4.09%, driven by inflation fears linked to the US-Israeli war on Iran. (Trading Economics)
- [2026-03-03] Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has been largely halted due to the war in Iran, boosting global freight and insurance costs. (Nasdaq)
- Broader cocoa market faces headwinds from a projected global surplus for the 2025/26 season. (RAW RESEARCH FINDINGS; note: source specificity limited)
[ANALYSIS]: Short-term price rebound (1.88%) reflects technical buying amid elevated global freight/insurance costs from Strait of Hormuz disruptions, which indirectly raise commodity supply chain expenses (though cocoa shipping from West Africa is less directly impacted). Countering this, USD strength (DXY 99.13) and higher Treasury yields (4.09%)—fueled by hawkish Fed signals and geopolitical inflation risks—deter non-US demand for dollar-denominated cocoa. Fundamental bearish pressure from 2025/26 surplus outlook persists. Overall, near-term dynamics remain balanced with offsetting cost-push inflation and demand suppression.
[CONFIDENCE]: 0.55
[SENTIMENT TAG]: [SENTIMENT: NEUTRAL]
